title: Introduction
text: You've likely heard of PageRank—it's the original Google algorithm that revolutionized web search. In this lesson, we'll apply it to our citation network to find the most influential papers. At its core, PageRank reveals which nodes matter most by measuring recursive influence. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s451-000-new] As you can see here, the illustration shows nodes of varying sizes representing different levels of influence, with highlighted paths indicating the flow of influence through the network.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to: Explain what PageRank measures and why it matters, describe the random surfer model and damping factor, run PageRank using the Python GDS client, interpret PageRank results and check for convergence, and find cross-disciplinary influential papers.

title: What PageRank Measures
text: PageRank doesn't simply count how many connections a node has. Instead, it measures something more nuanced: how many nodes point to you, how important those nodes are, and the overall 'quality' of your connections. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s453-001-new] Notice in this diagram how PageRank measures influence, with nodes of different sizes and colors representing varying importance and connection quality.

title: Quality Over Quantity
text: PageRank optimizes for quality over quantity. Being cited by an influential paper matters far more than being cited by an obscure one. A single citation from a foundational paper in your field can outweigh dozens of citations from lesser-known work.

title: The Random Surfer Model
text: To understand how PageRank works, imagine a researcher randomly browsing through papers in a library. Let's walk through what this researcher does, step by step.

title: Random Surfer: Step 1
text: First, our researcher starts at a random paper in the network. They've picked up a paper off the shelf without any particular goal in mind.

title: Random Surfer: Step 2
text: Next, most of the time—about 85% of the time with dampingFactor: 0.85—they follow a citation to another paper. They see an interesting reference, so they go find that paper and start reading it instead.

title: Random Surfer: Step 3
text: But occasionally—about 15% of the time with dampingFactor: 0.85—they get bored or distracted and jump to a completely random paper instead. This simulates the unpredictable nature of real browsing behavior.

title: Random Surfer: Step 4
text: Now imagine this researcher repeats this process millions of times. Over time, they'll visit some papers far more often than others.

title: What PageRank Represents
text: PageRank equals the probability of landing on each paper during this random browsing process. Papers that the researcher lands on frequently are considered influential—they're the papers that naturally attract attention through the network structure.

title: The Damping Factor
text: Remember how our random surfer sometimes jumps to a random paper instead of following citations? The probability of this random jump is controlled by a parameter called the **damping factor**.

title: Damping Factor Values
text: When we set `dampingFactor = 0.85`, we're saying that 85% of the time the surfer follows links, and 15% of the time they jump randomly. The damping factor must be between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s462-002-new] This flowchart shows the random surfer model with different damping factor values, illustrating the balance between link following and random jumps.

title: Damping Factor Effects
text: Higher damping values place more weight on the actual link structure of the graph. Lower damping values produce a more uniform distribution, where all nodes end up with similar scores. The default value of 0.85 works well for most graphs, so you rarely need to change it.

title: Why Do We Need Random Jumps?
text: The random jump mechanism isn't just a quirky detail—it actually solves three important problems that can break the algorithm. Let's look at each one. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s464-004-new] This diagram shows how random jumps prevent issues like spider traps, rank sinks, and dead-ends in PageRank calculations.

title: Problem 1: Spider Traps
text: Spider traps are groups of nodes with no outgoing links to the rest of the graph. Without random jumps, all the ranking signals would get 'trapped' and accumulate here, giving these nodes artificially high scores.

title: Problem 2: Rank Sinks
text: Rank sinks are cycles in the graph that accumulate rank infinitely. The ranking signals keep circling around and around, never escaping to the rest of the network. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s466-009-existing] This diagram illustrates a rank sink, where nodes form a cycle trapping PageRank scores.

title: Problem 3: Dead Ends
text: Dead ends are nodes with no outgoing relationships at all. When the random surfer reaches a dead end, the rank 'leaks' out of the graph entirely. Random jumps give them somewhere to go. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s467-010-existing] This diagram illustrates a dead end in a graph, where a node has no outgoing connections.

title: Why Not Just Count Citations?
text: You might wonder: why not just count how many citations each paper has? Let's look at an example to see why that approach falls short. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s468-005-new] This illustration compares simple citation counts with PageRank, highlighting differences in influence measurement.

title: Citation Count vs PageRank
text: In this comparison, we see that Paper A has 100 citations from obscure papers, resulting in a PageRank of 2.5. Paper B, with only 20 citations from foundational papers, has a much higher PageRank of 8.7. Paper C, with a mix of citations, has a PageRank of 4.2.

title: Importance, not popularity
text: Notice that Paper B has far fewer citations than Paper A, but a much higher PageRank score. Why? Because Paper B is cited by the right papers—foundational work that itself has high influence. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s470-011-existing] This diagram compares citation count to PageRank, showing how fewer citations can result in higher PageRank due to influential connections.

title: Now Let's Run PageRank
text: With the theory covered, let's apply PageRank to our citation network. First, we need to retrieve the graph projection we created in Lesson 5. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s471-006-new] This flowchart shows the steps to apply PageRank to a citation network, from graph retrieval to result interpretation.

title: Setup: Retrieve the Projection
text: We use `gds.graph.get()` to retrieve an existing projection by name. This allows us to access the graph structure we need for running PageRank.

title: Running PageRank: Write Mode
text: Now we can run PageRank. We'll use write mode so the results get persisted back to the database. This lets us query the results later using Cypher.

title: Inspecting the Results
text: The result object contains useful information about what the algorithm did. It tells us how many papers had their PageRank computed, how many iterations were run, and the total compute time.

title: Key Parameters
text: Here are the main parameters you can configure when running PageRank: The `dampingFactor` with a default of 0.85, `maxIterations` with a default of 20, `tolerance` with a default of 1e-7, and `writeProperty` which is required for storing results.

title: Understanding Convergence
text: PageRank is an iterative algorithm. It runs multiple passes over the graph, refining the scores each time. The algorithm stops when one of two things happens: the scores change less than `tolerance` between iterations, meaning it has converged, or it reaches `maxIterations`, meaning it ran out of time. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s476-012-existing] This illustration shows the concept of algorithm convergence in PageRank, with iterative passes refining scores.

title: Checking for Convergence
text: If the algorithm used all available iterations, it may not have fully converged. We can check this by comparing `ranIterations` to our `maxIterations` setting. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s477-011-new] This flowchart illustrates the process of checking for convergence in PageRank and steps to take if convergence is not achieved.

title: Re-running with More Iterations
text: If you find that PageRank has not converged, the fix is simple: increase `maxIterations` and run again. This allows the algorithm more time to refine the scores.

title: A Better Approach: Stats Mode First
text: Rather than writing results and then checking convergence, you can use stats mode to test your parameters first. Stats mode runs the algorithm and returns statistics, but doesn't store anything.

title: Finding the Most Influential Papers
text: Now that we have PageRank scores written to the database, we can query for the most influential papers. Let's find the top 10. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s480-014-new] This flowchart shows the process of querying the database for top influential papers based on PageRank scores.

title: Top Papers Results
text: Let's see which papers came out on top. We can display the results to identify the most influential works in our network.

title: Analyzing Influence by Subject
text: We can also aggregate PageRank scores by subject area to see which research fields have the most influence overall. This gives us insights into which areas are producing the most impactful work.

title: Subject Influence Results
text: This tells us which subject areas tend to produce more influential work. By displaying the subject influence, we can see the distribution of impact across different fields.

title: Cross-Disciplinary Papers
text: Some of the most interesting papers are those that bridge different research areas. These cross-disciplinary papers connect ideas across fields and often have outsized impact. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s484-016-new] This illustration depicts cross-disciplinary papers as bridges connecting different research areas.

title: Finding Cross-Discipline Citations
text: Let's find influential papers that cite work in subjects different from their own. This helps us identify papers that are making connections across different fields.

title: Papers Citing Multiple Subjects
text: We can take this further and find papers that cite across multiple different subject areas. These are potential interdisciplinary bridges—papers that connect several research communities.

title: Visualizing the Distribution
text: PageRank scores typically follow a power-law distribution. This means most nodes have relatively low scores, while a small number of nodes have very high scores. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s487-013-existing] This illustration shows the concept of power-law distribution in PageRank scores, with most nodes having low scores and a few having high scores.

title: Creating a Histogram
text: Let's visualize this distribution with a histogram of all PageRank scores in our network. This will help us see the overall distribution of influence.

title: Histogram Results
text: As expected, we see the characteristic long-tail distribution: many papers with low PageRank, and a few standout papers with high scores.

title: Interpreting Results: Relative Scores
text: One important thing to remember is that PageRank scores are **relative**, not absolute. Scores typically range from 0.15 to 20+ depending on the graph. You should only compare scores of nodes from the same run in the same graph. Higher scores indicate more influential or 'central' nodes. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s490-019-new] This diagram illustrates the concept of relative PageRank scores, showing how scores vary within the same graph and run.

title: Interpreting Results: Our Citation Network
text: In our citation network specifically: Papers with PageRank > 5 are highly influential in this network. Papers that cite multiple subject areas are interdisciplinary bridges. High PageRank combined with citations to multiple subjects suggests foundational interdisciplinary work.

title: Combining with Other Metrics
text: PageRank tells us an important part of the story, but not the whole story. For richer insights, combine PageRank with other metrics: **Citation count** gives you raw popularity, **Betweenness Centrality** shows bridges between communities (we'll cover this next), and **Community membership** reveals which cluster a paper belongs to.

title: Multi-Metric Analysis
text: By combining multiple metrics, you can identify papers that are influential, well-connected, and serve as bridges between research areas. This multi-dimensional view is far more powerful than any single metric alone. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s493-021-new] This diagram illustrates the multi-metric analysis approach, highlighting how different metrics complement each other.

title: Common pitfalls
text: Before we wrap up, let's cover three common mistakes people make when using PageRank.

title: Pitfall 1: Comparing across runs
text: PageRank scores are relative to the specific graph they were computed on and the specific PageRank run on that graph. A score of 5.0 in one graph means something completely different than a score of 5.0 in another graph. A score of 5.0 on the same graph but different runs might be okay—but run it in full again anyway. The random nature of the random surfer model will introduce inconsistencies across runs. Never compare PageRank scores across different graphs. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s495-023-new] This illustration shows why comparing PageRank scores across different graphs or runs is misleading.

title: Pitfall 2: Ignoring convergence
text: If `ranIterations` equals `maxIterations`, the algorithm may not have converged properly. Always check for this condition, and use stats mode first to verify your parameters before writing results. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s496-024-new] This flowchart shows the steps to verify convergence and adjust parameters if needed.

title: Pitfall 3: Damping factor extremes
text: The damping factor should stay close to the default of 0.85. Setting it too high—close to 1—causes the spider trap problems. Setting it too low makes all scores converge toward uniform values, losing the signal you're looking for. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-6-pagerank-deep-dive-s497-016-existing] This illustration shows the effects of extreme damping factor values, with one graph having spider traps and another showing uniformity.

title: Summary
text: In this lesson, you learned how PageRank measures recursive influence: Being cited by influential papers matters more than raw citation count. The random surfer model simulates browsing behavior to calculate influence. The damping factor prevents spider traps, rank sinks, and dead-ends. Always check convergence by comparing `ranIterations` to `maxIterations`. You applied PageRank to the citation network, found the most influential papers, and discovered cross-disciplinary bridges. **Next:** We'll use Betweenness Centrality to find 'bridge papers' that connect different research communities.
